region_of_origin: Mediterranean origin (Italy/Greece); now globally cultivated. In Singapore imported primarily from Australia and China.
food_subgroup: Cruciferous green vegetable, raw. Very low calorie (~34 kcal/100g). Very high vitamin C (~89 mg/100g). High folate (63 mcg/100g). Source of sulforaphane precursor glucoraphanin.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of raw broccoli is approximately 1 cup of florets, weighing about 90g, providing roughly 31 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Broccoli, Raw — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 170379)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw broccoli, FDC ID 170379. All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~15 for raw broccoli sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. GL calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 6.6 × 0.9) / 100 ≈ 0.6 per 90g serving. Near-negligible glycemic impact per realistic serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (33 mg/100g); high potassium (316 mg/100g) supporting vascular tone
hypertension.guidance: Raw broccoli is highly suitable for hypertension management: its very low sodium content (33 mg/100g) and high potassium (316 mg/100g) support a favourable Na:K ratio, while folate (63 mcg/100g) aids homocysteine metabolism relevant to cardiovascular risk reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~15) and GL (~0.6 per 90g serving); 2.6g dietary fibre slows glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw broccoli is excellent for Type 2 Diabetes management: GI ~15 and GL ~0.6 per serving produce negligible postprandial glucose response, while 2.6g dietary fibre per 100g slows glucose absorption from co-consumed carbohydrates, and sulforaphane (from glucoraphanin via myrosinase activation when eaten raw) has preclinical evidence for improving insulin sensitivity via Nrf2 pathway activation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.1g/100g), zero cholesterol, high vitamin C (89.2 mg/100g) and folate (63 mcg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw broccoli is cardioprotective: negligible saturated fat (0.1g/100g) and zero cholesterol pose no atherogenic risk, high vitamin C (89.2 mg/100g) supports endothelial function and antioxidant defence, folate (63 mcg/100g) supports homocysteine metabolism, and potassium (316 mg/100g) supports healthy blood pressure; warfarin users should maintain consistent broccoli intake due to vitamin K content (~101 mcg/100g).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sulforaphane (Nrf2/NF-κB pathway); high vitamin C for collagen synthesis; folate for methotrexate patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw broccoli is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis: sulforaphane activates Nrf2 and inhibits NF-κB reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine production, vitamin C (89.2 mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis and antioxidant defence, and folate (63 mcg/100g) is important for patients on methotrexate therapy; warfarin users should maintain consistent intake due to vitamin K.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Broccoli (Raw) is a cruciferous green vegetable (Brassica oleracea var. italica) consumed raw or cooked. Per 100g raw: 34 kcal, 2.8g protein, 0.4g fat, 6.6g carbohydrate, 2.6g dietary fibre. GI ~15 (very low), GL ~0.6 per 90g serving. Exceptionally rich in vitamin C (89.2 mg = ~149% adult RDA), folate (63 mcg), vitamin K (~101 mcg), and potassium (316 mg). Key bioactive: sulforaphane is produced from glucoraphanin ONLY when broccoli is chopped or chewed raw — the myrosinase enzyme is destroyed above ~60°C, so cooking eliminates sulforaphane production unless raw mustard powder is added to cooked broccoli to restore enzymatic conversion. Vitamin C is ~70% retained in stir-frying and only ~35% retained in boiling. Texture: firm, crisp florets with mild earthy-green flavour. Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. Warfarin patients should maintain consistent broccoli intake due to vitamin K content. In Singapore, broccoli is imported primarily from Australia and China and is available at all major supermarkets. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC 170379. Confidence: very high.

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170379, an approved primary source with exact match to raw broccoli. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are verified and directly matched. No estimation or analogy required.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100g) per USDA SR Legacy FDC 170379 — broccoli is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero (0.0 mcg/100g) per USDA SR Legacy — broccoli is a plant food and naturally contains no vitamin B12. Vitamin D is confirmed zero (0.0 mcg/100g) per USDA SR Legacy — raw broccoli contains negligible vitamin D. Trans fat is confirmed zero (0.0g/100g) per USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat (0.03g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.04g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 170379. Water content (89.3g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 170379. Vitamin K (~101 mcg/100g) is noted in the LLM description and health context but is not a mandatory per_100g template field; warfarin users should maintain consistent intake. Sulforaphane bioavailability is maximised when broccoli is consumed raw or when raw mustard powder is added to cooked broccoli. The food_subgroup in the input was truncated; full description restored in this record. Multiple is_default:true portions in input retained as distinct serving options; canonical serving set to 90g (1 cup florets).
